title: Today's AI Brief: Open-Source Coding Models and AI Policy
summary: A look at Nous Research's new open-source coding model and South Africa's AI policy landscape.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment
imageCaption: Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment
imageSourceTitle: Nous Research's NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI brief. We're covering some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Nous Research has just released an open-source coding model that's making waves. Their NousCoder-14B model is competitive with larger proprietary models, and it's backed by crypto venture firm Paradigm. This is a big deal because it shows that open-source AI can be just as powerful as proprietary models.

Nous Research's NousCoder-14B model is landing right in the middle of the Claude Code moment. For those who might not know, Claude Code is a highly-regarded coding model. The fact that NousCoder-14B is matching or exceeding its capabilities is no small feat. This could have significant implications for the future of coding and software development. Imagine having access to a powerful coding model that is open-source and free to use - it's a game-changer for developers and the tech industry as a whole.

Also making headlines is South Africa's draft AI policy. The country has a unique opportunity to leverage AI, but its current policy leaves much to be desired. It's a reminder that AI policy is still in its infancy, and countries are still figuring out how to navigate this complex landscape. It's interesting to see how different countries approach AI policy, and South Africa's story is a great example of the challenges and opportunities that come with it.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
